Please describe your research career to date
My developing record of research publications is complemented by extensive patient and public involvement and
engagement (PPIE) throughout my career. In the last 3 years, this has started to translate into co-developing my
own research projects and this fellowship application with patients and care workers.
At medical school I was a member of the Doubleday Centre for Patient Experience, who work to enhance medical
education through patient and the public partnership. In 2020 I was the first award winner of the Doubleday
Student Award and subsequently the youngest nominated trustee for the charity. In 2021, moved by the
experiences of my patients and addressing the communication challenges posed by facemasks, I started a
national social media campaign #thesmilebehindthemask with the Doubleday Centre. The campaign was highly
commended for the Outstanding public engagement initiative (national/international) and winner of the Patient and
Public Involvement and Engagement Award from the University of Manchester. I continue to work with the
Doubleday Centre most recently being a judge for the Royal Society of Medicine annual essay competition and
working with the Doubleday Medical Schools Collaboration in updating the General Medical Council’s guidance for
patient partnership in medical education.
During medical school, I completed an additional year through an intercalation, earning a master’s in public health
with Distinction. Based on my work as a public health intern at Sport England I was approached by the National
Lead for Adult Health and Wellbeing to work part-time at Public Health England to lead research on men’s sexual
health and completed this research through a national online cross-sectional survey. I engaged with stakeholders
including Men’s Health Forum and the Race Equality Foundation, designed and tested the survey and collected
data from over 3000 people. I analysed some of this data for my dissertation, exploring men’s attitude to health
information and exploring health literacy.
A key point in my career was when I met a patient with late-stage cancer whose ability to be part of shared-
decision making was impacted by low health literacy. This made it hard to build a holistic care plan and he sadly
died in hospital without his family.
After reflection and reading, I found the literature on healthcare professionals (HCP) understanding of their
patients’ health literacy was sparse and USA-focused. Therefore, I started a research project examining the
accuracy of HCPs’ estimations of their patients’ health literacy and numeracy. I sought appropriate support from
my consultant and became principal investigator. Over three years in my spare time whilst training as a junior
doctor, I completed PPIE, wrote the protocol and ethics application and recruited and led a team of 7 medical
students and an SHO to collect the data. This cross-sectional survey study measured and compared patients’
levels of health literacy and numeracy to HCPs’ estimations. The study found that 55.5% of HCPs’ health literacy
estimations were correct and 15.9% of HCPs’ health numeracy estimations were correct. The study also found that
83.8% of patients struggled to read a food label.
The findings have been disseminated in a range of ways including:
 Oral presentation at the 12th Congress of the International Federation for the Surgery of Obesity and
Metabolic Disorders – European Chapter 2024.
 Presentations at a national community of practice for clinical librarians and Grand Round at the study site.
 Presentations at the study site’s division board meetings leading to programmes of work to improve
appointment letters and patient information leaflets in relation to health literacy.
 Integration of the study’s findings into the trust’s mandatory training of new allied health professionals.
 Abstract accepted for oral presentation at the 2nd Global Summit on Health Literacy in September 2024.
 Public-facing social media posts by the host trust.
 An accepted manuscript for the Obesity Surgery Journal.
Through this experience I became interested in pursuing a PhD so I applied for a Public Health Academic Clinical
Fellowship (ACF). During the ACF I learnt and analysed the data in ‘R’, wrote up the results, disseminated my
findings, and learnt more about health literacy and working in academia. I built a cross-disciplinary network of
academics to support the proposed doctoral fellowship and took up other opportunities including teaching master’s
students in public health data science and have been appointed as a visiting lecture in the public health research
methods and epidemiology module. I organised the annual London public health registrar conference and was a
judge for abstract submissions for the Institute for Health Advancement’s international Annual Health Literacy
Conference. Integrating myself with a range of clinical academics has also led to me working with a heamatology
consultant and medical student completing a health literacy project on outpatient letters for a myeloma clinic.
Inspiration for PhD
My fellowship application is inspired by a patient who was being supported by a care worker. In researching the
area, I was surprised by the paucity of literature looking at health literacy in care workers and the impact of this on
service user health. It is important to me to give voice to carers and service users, not just as participants but in
shaping the PhD. To develop this proposal, I spent 2 weeks shadowing at Nightingale Hammerson care home. I
worked early, late and night shifts, supported with care and chatted with residents/families and care workers to
hear their ideas for research on health literacy. This PhD question comes directly from care workers and is best
described in their words “If you have a good discharge note that will give you good continual care” – Senior
HCA
Impact of the fellowship
This fellowship will support me to build a broad range of skills to continue a career in research. The fellowship will
support me to further develop my qualitative research skills and stretch myself into a new area of expertise -
artificial intelligence. Working at the interface between health and social care will be an invaluable experience,
helping me to discern my place in academia and how I might use these experiences to support better
understanding and cohesion between these areas in research and policy.

APPLICATION DETAILS.Plain English Summary of Research:
What is the problem?
Our population is ageing and more people have complex health conditions and live in care homes. When patients
leave hospital, the medical team writes a discharge summary about their hospital stay and what care and
medications they now need. It is an important way for doctors to tell the carers in the care home about the patient.
However, discharge summaries are often written with other doctors, like GPs, in mind and are often not easy to
understand and not that useful for care workers. This research aims to improve discharge summaries for care
workers to make sure that everyone gets the care that’s right for them when they go to care homes from hospitals.
How will the research help solve this problem?
The research project has 4 questions.
Q1: What is the ability of care workers to understand health information?
Health literacy and health numeracy, are the ability to use reading, writing and maths skills to use and understand
information about health. I will research what we already know about health literacy and numeracy levels in care
workers and compare this to nurses. I will then do a national survey of care workers with health literacy and
numeracy tests to understand what the range of ability is.
Q2: How easy are discharge summaries to understand and what do care workers and medical teams think of
them?
I will look at discharge summaries from 3 hospitals to understand how difficult they are to read and compare this to
the health literacy and numeracy levels we find in the survey. I will look at how easy they are to read and
understand and if the next steps are clear. I will also look at the difficulty of the maths related information and the
digital skills the reader needs to use, such as following a QR code. I will do focus groups with medical teams and
care workers to understand if they have different views on what to include in a discharge summary, and how they
would improve them.
Q3: How should we write discharge summaries?
I will present everything I have learnt in questions 1-2 to a group of experts and work with them to develop a ‘best
practice guide’ to write discharge summaries for care workers. Experts will include experts in their fields and
experts by their lived experience such as in medical education and social care experts, care home residents and
their families, care workers and junior doctors.
Q4: Can we use AI to write discharge summaries in language that is easier to understand for care workers?
Using what we have learnt from questions 1-3, I will train an AI (artificial intelligence) model to re-write the
discharge summary using everyday language. I will test these summaries, checking for mistakes or missing
information and through interviews get patients and care workers opinions of these AI-generated summaries.
How will different voices be included in the research?
The project question was created with care workers, residents and their families. How the research will be done
has been discussed with members of the public through a community group. There will be a group of academics,
care home residents and their families, care workers and doctors who will provide feedback throughout.
How will you share the research results?
I will share the results through scientific journals, conferences and an event with groups such as medical schools,
royal colleges who help to educate doctors and other healthcare professionals. I will also create a video to share
the learning with care workers.
APPLICATION DETAILS.Scientific Abstract:
Background
Medical discharge summaries (MDS) are a vital document, transferring information from the hospital to care teams
in residential care homes on discharge. They support safe, patient-centred care communicating information about
the patient’s admission and post-discharge care including medications. However, MDS are not fulfilling their role,
they are often aimed at other medical staff such as GPs, and so are poorly suited to the needs of adult social care
professionals (referred to as carers in this document) who are likely to have different expertise and lower health
literacy. “The discharge summary is not so clear… there is a lot of medical terminology… the responsibility
is on you to call the hospital and clarify” - carer.
Aim: To support improved care for residents discharged to care homes from hospital by optimising the content
and format of MDS so they are accessible to carers without a medical or nursing background.
WP1: Investigating levels of health literacy and numeracy (HLN) of carers
1A: A systematic review of studies comparing the HLN of carers and nurses.
1B: A national online cross-sectional survey of carers working in residential care to measure their health literacy,
health numeracy and ehealth literacy using a suite of validated measures.
WP2: A mixed methods study to explore the purpose, form, content and style of MDS
2A: MDS from three trusts will be analysed looking at how easy they are to read and understand, and the
mathematical proficiency and online skills needed to use them. The level of ability required will be compared to the
skills level demonstrated in the survey in 1B.
2B: I will conduct 4 focus groups with the medical team and carers to explore their views on MDS and
opportunities for improvement. I will also undertake a documentary analysis of key documents (e.g. medical
school curriculums).
WP3: Co-production of principles for writing MDS to meet the needs of care workers
A range of experts and experts by experience including carers, residents/families, junior doctors and medical
educators will be recruited to co-produce principles for writing MDS using a modified Delphi method.
WP4: Testing the feasibility of generating lay summaries using natural language processing (NLP)
algorithms
In collaboration with machine learners at UCL, I will train an NLP algorithm, using the principles from WP3 and
then test the AI models’ ability to generate accessible lay summaries for carers from existing MDS.
Patient and Public Involvement
This project was co-produced with residents and carers and the methodology reviewed by the public considering
its aims and how to improve the methodology to ensure it’s accessible and inclusive for a range of participants.
The study will be supported by a steering committee including carers, residents and their families.
Impact
The co-produced principles will support more effective communication through MDS, facilitating safe, effective and
personalised care for residents on discharge to care homes. This will support reducing workloads for carers and
reducing the likelihood of medical events and hospital readmissions through unclear care plans and so have
costing saving benefits for the NHS.
APPLICATION DETAILS.Detailed Research Plan:
1. What is the problem being addressed?
Opportunities to support effective transition of care between leaving hospital and entry/return to care homes are
being missed due to poor communication between health and social care professionals. Medical discharge
summaries (MDS) are the key document in the handover process from hospital to other settings including social
care. Their purpose is to summarise the hospital admission and communicate recommendations for future care
(including medications) after discharge. However, MDS are usually written by junior doctors who may have limited
training and are primarily designed to communicate information to other medical staff such as GPs. They are often
poorly suited to the needs of professionals in adult social care (referred to as carers or care workers in this
document) who are likely to have different expertise and lower health literacy.
This study covers the strategic themes of social care, public health and health data science and will address how
MDS can be reformulated to better support the handover of care and information between hospitals and care
homes to inform the delivery of safe, person-centred care.
“If you have a good discharge note that will give you good continual care” – carer
2. Why is this research important in terms of improving the health and/or wellbeing of the public and/or to
patients/service users, carers and health and care services?
This research is important because the number of older people with complex health conditions living in care
homes is increasing.(1) MDS are an important part of ensuring a safe and patient-centred transition of care on
discharge, which will benefit individual health and the health and social care system. This research also addresses
health inequalities by focusing on older people and the social care sector.
Our population is ageing, in 2021, there were 278, 946 older people (>65) living in care homes across England
and Wales, with an expected increase in care homes residents of 127% over the next 20 years.(2,3) The
population is increasingly co-morbid with complex health needs, stretching the health knowledge and skills of care
workers.(4,5) In the final quarter of 2018/19, in England, there were 88,487 discharges to care homes; it is
therefore vital to optimise the transition of care between hospitals and care homes. (6)
We know that good handovers can improve patient safety, prevent adverse events, ensure continuity of care and
reduce hospital readmissions. (7,8) MDS can support good handovers, however the current way in which many
MDS are written, mainly aimed at other doctors, are a barrier to understanding and increase workload for care
workers. “The discharge summary is not so clear… the responsibility is on you to call the hospital and
clarify… it is not clear if medications have stopped or if there are changes” - carer.
This study will improve the handover processes by understanding the health literacy and numeracy of carers,
analysing the content and purpose of MDS from the perspective of carers, and providing guidance for how to re-
formulate MDS to make them accessible and utilisable to carers.
This research will also support ongoing efforts to address the need for more research in social care. The
collaborative approach built into the study’s methodologies will ensure that research is done with and not to carers
and care home residents, empowering them to actively shape outputs and recommendations.
3. Review of existing evidence - How does the existing literature support this proposal?
There is limited literature on the health literacy and numeracy (HLN) skills of care workers and how that relates to
the demands of their role. Evidence internationally is sparse but demonstrate that over a third of care workers may
have inadequate health literacy. (9–11) The evidence demonstrates that in the general population HLN skills are
low and a falling number of care workers have specialist qualifications in their field. In the UK, 71.4% the working
age population has a level 2 qualification or greater (equivalent to GCSE or higher) but only 57% have the health
literacy skills to understand and use of everyday health information, falling to 39% for health numeracy skills.(12)
Only 42% of care workers have a level 2 qualification or above in social care and this has fallen by 7% since
2017/18. (13) This is further compounded by high staff turnover in the care sector and increasing focus on
international recruitment.(14) Therefore, further evidence is needed to describe the HLN of care workers in order
to understand how to best write MDS that meets their needs. In this study this will be achieved through work
programme (WP) 1 through a systematic review looking at existing evidence of the HLN level of carers in social
care settings internationally and a national cross-sectional survey of care workers HLN skills in England.
To support the transition of care between hospital and care homes MDS must be understandable, readable and
contain the right information for the reader. Several research studies have highlighted the risks of
miscommunication at the interface between hospitals and social care, with too little information or missing
information in MDS. (15) MDS have scored poorly on tests measuring how difficult text is to read and understand
with scores ranging from 7.6-17, consistently exceeding the recommended score of less than 6, categorising many
MDS as difficult to read. (16–19) In my time at Nightingale Hammerson care home, care workers echoed this to
me.
“The discharge summary is not so clear… there is a lot of medical terminology… “, “A bit more user
friendly… so if it’s going out of the medical environment… what is CXR… they shouldn’t use acronyms at
all…”- carer
Evidence and testimonial demonstrate that further work is needed to understand and describe how discharge
summaries can meet the health literacy and information needs of care workers. This study through WP2 will
analyse MDS across 3 trusts in London and Manchester to gather England-based data on the readability of MDS
and conduct interviews with medical teams and care workers to describe the content that needs to be included in
MDS to meet the needs of adult social care.
The majority of MDS are written by junior doctors who are often in their first two years of training and it is felt that
they may not have had adequate training on writing them.(20,21) There is also variability in MDS templates
between different trusts. WP3 will co-produce guidance and resources for the medical team that will improve their
understanding of the needs of adult social care.
Finally, it is important to consider how findings can be implemented without adding additional burden to junior
doctors by leveraging artificial intelligence (AI). Previous studies have tried writing more accessible discharge
summaries by training AI using hundreds of examples MDS; this is time consuming and not reliable. (Tuan et al.,
2023) However, existing literature has now shown the ability of AI to create lay summaries of scientific literature
without this type of time-consuming training. In WP4 this study will trial a scalable solution where the AI can be
taught through a method called prompting and will only need to be given one example, with the aim to write a lay
language summary as an adjunct to existing MDS.
What is the research question / aims and objectives?
Aim: To support improved care for residents discharged to care homes from hospital by optimising the content
and format of MDS (discharge summaries) so they are accessible to carers without a medical or nursing
background.
Objectives:
1) To investigate levels of health literacy and numeracy (HLN) in carers working in social care settings
by:
a. Undertaking a systematic review to estimate and compare the levels of HLN based on published
data in nurses and care workers (WP1)
b. Surveying care workers to measure levels of health literacy, numeracy and ehealth literacy.
(WP1)
2) To use mixed methods to explore the purpose, form, content and style of discharge summaries
by:
a. Quantitatively assessing the complexity of information in a sample of discharge summaries from 3
hospitals (WP2)
b. Undertake focus groups with medical teams (who write discharge summaries) and care workers
(who are the recipients of discharge summaries) (WP2)
3) To build consensus around a set of principles for writing MDS that are accessible to care workers,
considering their levels of health and ehealth literacy, numeracy and their information needs (WP3)
4) To test the feasibility of incorporating these principles into AI tools (based on language models) that
automate production of lay summaries from existing MDS (WP4)
5. Project Plan
This project will be hosted within the UCL-led Vivaldi (https://www.ucl.ac.uk/health-informatics/research/vivaldi)
which has an established network of older adult care homes and strong relationships with care providers,
residents, relatives and care home staff to support research delivery. The study will also be supported by the
London Health and Care in the Community programme (HCIC) and their regional network of care homes and
social care partners. Access to discharge summaries and medical teams will be facilitated by existing links to 3
trusts and the London enhance programme, a postgraduate education network for healthcare professionals. Care
workers work in diverse environments, this project will focus on care workers in non-nursing residential care
homes.
WP1: Investigating levels of health literacy and numeracy of carers working in social care
1A: Systematic review
Study question: What is the HLN of carers in health and social care settings, focusing on care for older people?
Methodology: I will complete a systematic review of observational studies of HLN of nurses and carers working in
health and social care settings. I will use a range of databases including PubMed, ERIC and search grey literature
and will work with a library information specialist to refine my search terms. I will follow PRISMA guidelines and
register the protocol with PROSPERO.
Primary outcome: level of HLN assessed using a variety of scoring systems of carer workers.
Analysis: The quality of studies will be assessed using STROBE and the results analysed through narrative
synthesis.
1B: Quantitative assessment of the health literacy, numeracy and ehealth literacy of carers and registered
managers in residential care homes
Aim: To measure and describe the health literacy, numeracy and ehealth literacy of care workers working in
residential care homes.
Study design: cross-sectional survey
Setting: Online
Methodology:
Participants will complete a one-off online survey. The survey will measure health literacy, numeracy and ehealth
literacy using three validated tools: the Functional Health literacy test (FHLT), the Numeracy Understanding in
Medicine Instrument- short form (S-NuMI) and the eHealth Literacy Scale (eHEALS) respectively and participants
will be asked to rate their confidence in the online skills identified in the discharge summaries in WP2. I will
measure HL, HN and eHL separately rather than using combined measures. These tools have been chosen as
they are self-administered, focus on general health knowledge and written communication to reflect the breadth
and medium of discharge summaries. Covariates including demographics, workplace and training-related factors
will also be collected. The survey will be administered via RedCap, an established research data collection service
commissioned by UCL which stores data securely within the UCL data safe haven. Participants will provide
electronic consent prior to completing the questionnaire and REDCap will segregate the consent form from the
survey for storage.
Recruitment will be supported through the national Vivaldi network and in-person promotion supported by HCIC
London. This will support carers from different care homes across the country to be involved and participants will
be provided a £15 voucher incentive.
Inclusion/exclusion criteria:
Current care professionals, in England, who need to read MDS to provide direct care or to disseminate information
to those who provide direct care predominantly in older age residential non-nursing care settings and who have
completed their organisation’s induction process will be included.
Social care professionals with a healthcare background such as occupational therapists, physiotherapists and
medical and nursing students will be excluded.
Sample size
To identify the proportion of care professionals with functional health literacy, 156 surveys will need to be
completed by care professionals to achieve a 95% confidence interval with a margin of error of 5%, assuming a
true proportion of 88.6% (22). Target recruitment will be 180 to account for incomplete questionnaires.
Analysis: data will be analysed in R. The results will be analysed descriptively and analysed statistically for an
association between skills and demographic and workplace factors. The skills-based results of the questionnaire
will be cross-referenced against the level of literacy and numeracy difficulty in the MDS measured in WP2 to make
inferences about carers ability to access this information in its current format.
WP2: A mixed methods study to explore the purpose, form, content and style of MDS
2A: Quantitative assessment of the content of discharge summaries
Aim: To measure the readability, actionability and understandability of MDS, numeracy difficulty level and
document the type of online skills required to use an MDS.
I will assess the content of MDS by looking at how easy it is to read (readability), how well the information can be
understood and acted on (understandability and actionability), the level of the maths skills involved when reading
and using the information (numeracy) and the skills involved in accessing any online information sign-posted on
the MDS (online skills).
Study design: documentary analysis
Setting: 3 English trusts
Methodology:
291 MDS for older adults being discharged to a care home on randomly selected day(s) will be analysed.
Discharge summaries will be collected from 3 trusts, capturing a diverse set of centrally located teaching hospitals
and smaller district general hospitals in the south and north of the country. Information sharing agreements and
feasibility has already been agreed with all three trusts, Whittington NHS Trust, University College London Hospital
(UCLH) and Manchester Foundation Trust.
Inclusion and exclusion criteria for discharge summaries:
MDS for patients >65 years old being discharged to a non-nursing residential care home will be included. MDS not
written in English, relating to in-hospital deaths or inter-hospital transfer and other forms of letter communications
such as outpatient letters will not be included. MDS from the five most common hospital specialties experienced by
foundation doctors will be included, as the majority of discharge summaries are written by foundation doctors.
(23,24)
Outcomes and covariates
The primary outcome will be readability. Secondary outcomes will include numeracy, understandability and
actionability. Characteristics of the MDS will be extracted including: patient’s age, sex, ethnicity, length of stay,
type of admission, usual place of residence prior to admission, specialty, and Trust.
Readability
Readability scores for MDS will be measured using Microsoft Word’s Flesch-Kincaid tool. The Fleisch-Kincaid tool
has been chosen to maintain consistency across the work programmes as the health literacy questionnaire text is
also measured using this tool. The tool is well-known, widely accessible through Word and reproducible which will
make the findings, methodology and possible recommendations easier to utilise, reproduce and access by
stakeholders or future researchers.
Understandability and actionability
Understandability and actionability of MDS will be assessed using the Patient Education Materials Assessment
Tool (PEMAT). The PEMAT is designed for patient education materials and has been used previously to analyse
MDS.(25) PEMAT provides a tool to explore how MDS relate to the information needs of the reader. The results of
the PEMAT are numerical.
Numeracy level
I will assess MDS complexities in relation to numeracy skills. I will identify where the MDS involve numerical
information, calculations or data interpretation such as medication dosages and laboratory tests. I will use the adult
numeracy core curriculum as a rating tool to evaluate the difficulty of the numeracy-related information in the MDS.
This will allow me to determine the mathematical proficiency required to understand and use MDS. To ensure the
robustness of my ratings I will prepare mock examples and medications lists to review with Dr Mallows from the
Institute of Education, UCL.
Finally, I will record what skills carers will need to access additional online resources that are signposted in the
MDS. Examples include using a QR code or following a URL.
Sample size calculation
Sample size is calculated for each trust individually, to take account of likely variation between different trusts’
MDS. 97 discharge summaries for each trust would provide a minimum level of precision of +/- 10%, using a 95%
confidence interval, to assess the proportion of discharge summaries that have an appropriate readability level of
Flesch-Kincaid score >60.
Analysis
Measures will be summarised using descriptive statistics. I will also test for association between covariates and
each of the listed outcomes.
2B: Qualitative assessment through focus groups
Aim: To explore the differences between the views of social care professionals and medical teams on the role of
MDS.
Research question: What are the differences between what medical teams and social care professionals see as
the role of MDS and does this lead to differences in their expectations of its contents?
Study design: documentary analysis and focus groups
I will explore carers’ views on the role of MDS and how they can effectively fulfil this role and compare this to the
medical community’s understanding of the roles of MDS through focus groups and the analysis of educational
materials and guidelines.
Methodology:
I will conduct two carer focus groups and two separate medical team focus groups, consisting of 6-8 participants
each, which can be supplemented by 1:1 or smaller group or pair interviews where timetabling prevents
participation. I will aim for a minimum of 12 carers and 12 medical team members. Where practical, interviews will
be held face-to-face or otherwise over MS Teams.
Recruitment:
Carers: participants taking part in WP1 will be asked if they are happy to be contacted to participate in a focus
group. Opportunities to participate in the focus group will also be advertised via NIHRs’ Enabling Research in Care
Homes (ENRICH) networks, adult social care networks supported by HCIC, Vivaldi and the Outstanding Society
nationally. Participants will be purposively selected to maximise diversity in terms of health literacy skill, care
homes size and CQC ratings, region and demographic factors. Inclusion and exclusion criteria will reflect that of
WP1. Participants information sheets and consent forms will be shared ahead of interviews and signed at the
beginning of interviews electronically. To support engagement carers’ time will be compensated with a e-gift card
of £25, and travel costs covered.
Medical team: recruitment will be through the London Enhance programme and trusts supporting WP1. Doctors or
other healthcare professionals responsible for writing MDS for older age adult patients will be eligible. Participants
information sheets and consent forms will be shared ahead of interviews and signed at the beginning of interviews
electronically.
Data collection: A topic guide will be created with feedback from the steering committee. All interviews will be
audio recorded and transcribed for analysis.
Documentary analysis: I will complete a documentary analysis, this will include the UK medical school
curriculums of focus group participants and other resources or guidance described by the participants of the
medical team focus group. Medical school curriculums will be accessed with the support of the Doubleday
Medical Schools Patient Partnership Collaboration.
Outcome of interest:
 References in the materials to the role of MDS.
 References in the material to the expected, intended or possible audiences of the MDS.
 Summaries of what to include in the MDS.
 Reference to the type of language to be used when writing MDSs.
Analysis:
I will analyse the findings from the focus groups and collated documents using thematic analysis. I will create the
themes using an inductive process from codes. I will follow the commonly adopted six step process described by
Braun and Clarke. NVivo will be used to support analysis.(26)
Work programme 3
Aim: To co-produce principles through consensus development methods to support the writing of MDS to meet
the needs of adult social care.
Study design: Modified Delphi method
Methodology: Using learnings from WP1 and WP2 I will create a set of principles to support the formulation of an
accessible MDS for carers. I will take advice from the steering committee on how to present these principles, e.g. a
best practice guide, a standard or a toolkit. I will recruit around 30 experts and experts by experience to reflect the
breadth of MDS users and writers to ensure the principles do not alienate any group. These experts will include
care workers, care home residents and families, informal carers, patients and healthcare professionals. I will use
up to three rounds to build consensus and participants will be asked to rate/rank each principle or element and
provide free text comments, depending on the decided format of the principles. Documents and responses will be
shared via email. Documents will be available in plain English to support accessibility and responses collected
face-to-face as needed for residents/families. Once these are collected and analysed, points not reaching a
consensus of >75% will be sent for further comment. At the end of 3 rounds if consensus is not met participants
will be invited to a consensus meeting, which depending on availability may be online, face-to-face or hybrid.
(27,28)
Work programme 4
Aim: To test the feasibility of generating accurate and readable lay summaries and the acceptability of summaries
generated through natural language processing algorithms.
Study design: Pre- post- comparative evaluation
Setting: UCLH
Methodology: Large generative pretrained transformer language models such as GPT-4 have shown good
performance in language interpretation and question-answering tasks. I will test their ability to generate a lay
version of a MDS that is suitable for carers by incorporating the principles developed through WP3. It is the
intention that these lay summaries would be read alongside the original doctor-written summaries.
I will work with the natural language processing team at the Institute of Health Informatics, who are already
working on the automation of MDS writing, to turn the principles from WP3 into instructions, known as prompts, for
AI to write lay summaries. I will use the model ‘LlaMa’ as it is currently being integrated into UCLH system. I will
use 5 anonymised discharge summaries and an example of the desired outcome to help develop and refine the
instructions given to the model. I will anonymise 10 further discharge summaries and feed these to LlaMA to
create AI-generated lay summaries.
Analysis:
I will adopt a mixed-methods approach to evaluate the AI-version of the summary compared to the original. First, I
will apply the measures from WP2 to compare the readability, understandability, actionability and numeracy level
of the original and AI-generated summaries. The accuracy and completeness of the clinical information and any
information inserted by the AI but not present within the original document will be assessed for by a clinician.
Next, I will conduct ten 1:1 interviews with multiple care professionals, care home residents/families and working
age patients to assess the acceptability and utility of AI-generated summaries. Participants will be recruited
through UCLH, Vivaldi, the Outstanding Society, the HCIC network and ENRICH. Interviews will be transcribed
and analysed using thematic analysis with themes arising inductively.
Dissemination, outputs and anticipated impact
The findings of each WP will be disseminated through scientific publications and conference submissions. Where
end users are outside the medical field such as Skills for Care grey-literature reports and infographics will also be
used to support dissemination. I will present findings at a range of forums such as social care forums and the
Doubleday Medical Schools Collaboration. I will co-lead a knowledge share event alongside care workers from the
steering committee, to share learnings with relevant stakeholders and create a space in which a diverse range of
stakeholders have the opportunity to create solutions within their area of expertise and build awareness and
commitment.
Outputs
WP1A: Evidence summarising levels of HLN in nurses and carers in health and social care settings.
End-users: care providers and organisations that provide training to care workers e.g. Skills for Care
WP1B: A measure of the health literacy, numeracy, ehealth literacy and digital skills of carers, in England.
End-users: Stakeholders in adult social care workforce development
The HLN and eHL of carers is unknown. Providing an understanding of its distribution will be valuable to
stakeholders who provide educational resources or training to this population, to ensure that information is
appropriately delivered. Measures of eHL and digital skills will be supportive to the implementation of the
government’s plan to increase the use of technology in the care sector.
WP1-3: Guidance and educational materials to support the teaching and dissemination of the co-created principles
from WP3 for medical teams and complementary materials for carers.
End-users: Stakeholders in undergraduate and postgraduate medical education; NHS trusts; Royal Colleges.
WP4: Code or prompts that can be used by AI researchers or developers to write MDS and more broadly health
information for lay audiences.
End-users: The co-produced principles and code produced in WP4 will provide an exemplar that can be built on by
computer-scientist as AI develops.
Anticipated Impact
Impact on patients
Improving the content and accessibility of MDS will benefit patient safety and health outcomes. Enhancing this key
handover document will support a smoother transition post-discharge, ensuring clear communication both to care
homes and has the potential for a wider benefit to patients being discharged to their own homes.
Impact for adult social care
MDS that meet the needs of carers will support safe and person-centred transition of care on discharge and will
help to reduce workload as information is shared in a timely and effective manner. A further aim of the study is to
empower carers on the steering committee through additional training tailored to their individual development
needs, this may include presentation skills, training to understand research methods, facilitator skills or mentoring.
The overall aim is to prepare them to co-lead the knowledge share event and provide an opportunity to develop
skills to continue as champions for social care beyond this project such as in research or with policy makers.
Impact for the NHS in secondary and community care
Improved patient safety and a better supported transition to social care will reduce the burden on the NHS. It has
the opportunity to reduce re-admissions and improve health outcomes by ensuring that instructions and advice can
be followed. For individual healthcare professionals in secondary care and community this reduces day-to-day
work burden if MDS are clear and do not require further explanation. For the wider NHS systems improved
adherence and reduced re-admissions will have a beneficial cost savings due to reduced demand.
Oversight and Governance
Steering committee
The steering committee will meet throughout the study as demonstrated in the GANTT chart, with additional ad-
hoc meetings to draw on particular expertise. It will be made up of care professionals, residents/family members,
members of the medical team, stakeholders from medical education, experts from within (workplace/adult)
education and NHS community care colleagues and be chaired by myself.
Project Management
I will use a GANTT chart to plan the project and will review the timelines and risk register quarterly to ensure the
research is on target. I will monitor the expenditure with the UCL IHI Finance Team and this will also sit within the
risk register. The project and timelines will be supported by a thesis committee who will meet 5 times throughout
the PhD.
I will meet with my primary supervisor Prof Shallcross fortnightly and Dr Holly Walton monthly and with Prof
Rowlands and Dr Anoop Shah fortnightly during WP1/3 and WP4 respectively.
The whole supervisory team and steering committee will also meet at key points as outlined in the GANTT chart
with additional ad-hoc meetings to draw on particular areas of expertise. Shared ways of working will be agreed
with members of the steering committee.
Finally, the success of the project and the aims of the fellowship is dependent on continuous reflective practice. I
will dedicate a day each month for reflection on the PhD progress, personal development and by taking gemba
walks spending time on-the-floor with the key stakeholders to reground myself such as in the care home or with
the discharge or medical team. This second style of reflective practice will be especially important in helping me to
connect with the needs of my stakeholders.
Ethics
Ethical approvals will be submitted in stages and UCL will be the sponsor. To measure the HLN of carers in WP1
and conduct focus groups in WP2 I will seek approval from the UCL Research Ethics Committee (REC). To
analyse and use MDS in WP2 and WP4 I will seek approval from the NHS REC. To work with care home
residents/their families as experts by experience in WP3 I will seek approval from an NHS REC specialising in
Social Care.

APPLICATION DETAILS.Patient & Public Involvement:
Please describe how patients/service users, carers and the public have been involved in developing this
proposal
Co-production is at the heart of the project and has been a cornerstone since its inception.
As a doctor, I recognise my limited experience of social care and the importance of spending time with patients
and the public and what we can learn from their lived experience.
Nightingale Hammerson kindly welcomed me into one of their homes. I spent 2 weeks with them working early,
night and day shifts. I spoke with carers, residents and families to understand the needs relating to health literacy
and what they felt could be improved through research. It was on a night shift that one of the carers came up with
the aim of the PhD and I discussed the idea with residents and their families who agreed on the importance of
MDS for carers. “Some patients are not capable of understanding their condition very well… but a copy [of
the MDS] should also be sent to the home” – resident.
I then worked with a range of stakeholders representing, care home residents, social care professionals and junior
doctors to adapt and refine the methodology. I also spoke to researchers who have worked in social care to
understand their recommendations on best supporting engagement with residents and carers in research.
I took my proposed methodology to a community group, mainly compromised of older age adults. They agreed
with the aims and intended research output and could reflect from their own experiences that improvements in the
accessibility of MDS would be beneficial for both social care and patients more broadly. From our discussion I
adapted the Delphi methodology as they felt that Teams or emails would be a barrier for many older people,
especially those in care homes. Instead, I will provide the option for printed versions and then go back to discuss
and note down their thoughts face-to-face. I will also provide example MDS to help contextualise the project and
stimulate thoughts. I will make similar adaptations for residents/family members on the steering committee.
I worked with one of the members of the community group to check the accessibility of the plain English summary.
Please describe the ways in which patients/service users, carers and the public will be actively involved in
the proposed research, including any training and support provided
Care home residents and the public have already played a vital role in setting the question and supporting
methodology development. Care home residents/family members and the public will continue to advise on the
running of the research through the steering committee which will influence the work from protocol to
dissemination, with 7 planned meetings across the 3 years in addition to ad hoc meetings. Residents/family
members and the public will be supported to fully take part in the steering committee by:
 Pre-meetings to allow them to ask questions to build confidence for the main meeting.
 The option for papers to be printed and posted instead of reading them online – in a print that is
comfortable for them to read.
 Offer of technical accessories to support taking part e.g headphones, webcam, Microsoft Word Package.
 The steering group’s terms of reference (TOR) will highlight the importance of all voices being heard.
 This is a cross-sector project bringing experts with lived experience, from education, health literacy,
medicine and adult social care with everyone having their own specialist language, the TOR will commit to
using plain English.
 Care home residents can participate alongside a family member with both renumerated for their time.
As part of my PDP, I will attend a facilitator course to support me in chairing the steering committee and ensuring
all voices are heard.
Having spoken to the community group and care home residents, I recognize that despite these adaptations taking
part in an online meeting may be challenging particularly for the care home residents. I will therefore also have the
option to visit the care home residents face-to-face before each meeting and allow their thoughts to be
incorporated in this way.
Although care workers are not PPI, their voice is often missing from the literature. I will take steps to empower
them both to contribute to this research and empower them to actively work with researchers or policy makers in
the future. Therefore, they will also receive coaching to support confidence, understanding of the academic
process and presentation skills.
If it is considered not appropriate and meaningful to actively involve patients/service users, carers and the
public in your proposed research, please justify why
N/A
APPLICATION DETAILS.Training & Development and Research Supp:
Proposed training and development programme
My training and development programme will support me to develop the skills I need for the PhD and for my future
academic career. UCL has a strong record of supporting PhD students and have a comprehensive Doctoral Skills
Development Programme (DSDP) to support development.
1. Systematic review (WP1A)
During my Master’s in Public Health I completed a module with Distinction in Evidence Synthesis:
systematic review. I will refresh my knowledge with a two 1-day courses delivered by the UCL DSDP.
2. Online survey (WP1B)
My previous experience in online and face-to-face survey-based research provides a strong foundation for
WP1. I will complete training in questionnaire design with the Social Research Association (SRA) (the-
sra.org.uk) and in RedCap, the online survey platform, through UCL DSDP.
3. Statistical analysis skills (WP1B)
As part of my Academic Clinical Fellowship, I undertook online courses in the statistical package ‘R’ and
applied these skills in my health literacy study. I will undertake a 3-hour training session by the UCL DSDP
to develop further skills in data visualisation in ‘R’ to support written dissemination.
4. Conducting focus groups and 1:1 interviews (WP2/4)
I previously conducted 1:1 interviews and analysis as part of a needs assessment. I will further my
knowledge in conducting online interviews through the ‘Digital qualitative interviews’ day course and
through a short course delivered by UCL ‘Qualitative Research Methods in Health’
(https://www.ucl.ac.uk/short-courses/search-courses/qualitative-research-methods-health).
5. Qualitative data analysis and write-up (WP2)
During my Master’s in Public Health I completed a module in ‘Qualitative Research Methods’. I will build on
this with four 1-day courses on the NVivo analysis package and skills in documentary analysis delivered
by UCL DSDP, SRA and the National Centre for Research Methods.
6. Writing best practice principles for writing MDS (WP3)
I will complete a Virtual Plain English Diploma Course with the Plain English campaign to improve the
writing style of final guidance documents. (https://www.plainenglish.co.uk/services/training/plain-english-
diploma.html) This will also provide key skills for further work in health literacy in the future.
7. Skills in using artificial intelligence model Llama (WP4)
Alongside the support I will receive from the natural language processing team, I will complete courses to
better understand the AI model. This is a rapidly developing field, and I will continue to review the best
course dependent on the version of the model, with promising courses available from DeepLearning.AI.
Developing broader skills for academia
1. Facilitator skills
As a public health registrar, I have developed skills in chairing and working with diverse stakeholders. I will
enhance these skills through a 5-day facilitator skills course delivered by the association of facilitators
(https://www.associationoffacilitators.co.uk/courses/group-dynamics.html). This will support me during the
focus groups in WP2/4, consensus meetings in WP3 and chairing the steering committee. It will also be an
important skill in a future career working between academia, public health and social care.
2. Patient and Public Involvement (PPI)
As a national ambassador for the Doubleday Centre for Patient Experience I have worked with a range of
patients, public and carers to enhance medical education. I will draw on these experiences during the PhD
and attend PPI training from UCL DSDP to transfer these skills to research. This training will cover the role
of PPI in research, running PPI focus groups and keeping patient partners involved.
3. Skills to support dissemination of findings
I have previous experience of disseminating research findings through the EPIC study. This included
written communication through blogs, social media posts, journal papers and academic posters and
through presentations at conferences, Grand Round and board meetings. I will build on this by taking a 1-
day course on infographic design delivered by the SRA and a 1-day storytelling course delivered by the
chartered institute of public relations
(https://www.cipr.co.uk/CIPR/Events/Current_Event_Display_New_Layout.aspx?EventKey=STO030424&
TrainingCode=STO).
4. Creating skills for the PhD journey and beyond
I will take part in a range of sessions provided by the UCL DSDP including:
 The PhD Journey
 Nature Masterclass: Persuasive grant writing
 Balancing competing commitments for academics and researchers
 Research careers
 Building your research pipeline and visibility
 The Writing Series: writing and publishing a research paper
Conferences
 I will attend the Global Health Literacy Summit anticipated to next be in 2027 and the International Health
Literacy conference in 2026. This will give me the opportunity to learn more about health literacy and to
present the findings of the research.
 I will attend the biennial UCL Qualitative Health Research Network conference which will provide me with
the opportunity to hear and learn about a breadth of qualitative research methods.
Coaching and Leadership Development
Dave Thornton is an executive coach and facilitator, having previously worked in senior leadership and
development in the NHS. His currently coaches CEO’s, Medical Directors, and individuals working in national
positions. Having learnt about my PhD project and after meeting me Dave offered to provide me coaching pro
bono to support the project and my personal development as a future leader.
Coaching to support care workers to be active participants in the steering committee
Emma Falkner has 22 years of experience in teaching and training. She is an expert in action learning, coaching
and facilitation and has previously worked with the care sector. Emma will support the care workers on the
steering committee to be fully active committee members. Across the PhD they will be mentored and provided
development opportunities to build confidence in speaking in meetings, public speaking such as at the knowledge
share event, reading board papers and being part of a writing team for reports or journals. As she works with the
care workers Emma will also support my development by providing me feedback, giving me an insight into my
leadership style and how I can create inclusive environments that support individual development.
Summary
My PDP will support the skills needed to complete each of the work programmes, building on existing training from
my Master’s, Academic Clinical Fellowship and public health registrar training. And will support my development
towards a career in academia through supporting the development of academic and professional leadership skills.

University College London Hospital (UCLH)
I will be based at the Institute of Health Informatics at UCL which works closely with UCLH and partners via the
Computational Medicine theme of the UCLH Biomedical Research Centre. This will ensure I have access to the
required AI/data science expertise in WP4 and will enable access to hospital discharge summaries for WP2 and
WP4.
Whittington NHS Trust and Manchester Foundation Trust
In preparation for the project, I have spoken to the research managers at Manchester Foundation Trust and
Whittington NHS trust who have both provided letters of support for the PhD and have agreed to support the PhD,
particularly WP2 to ensure access to discharge summaries.
Expertise in Adult Literacy and numeracy (UCL Institute of Education)
Dr Mallows is an Associate Professor at the UCL Institute of Education with experience and research in adult
education and adult literacy and numeracy. Dr Mallows has provided support on the methodology consideration in
the PhD and brings an additional perspective outside of health and social care from an educationalist’s
perspective. This will strengthen the final output of the work, considering both the information we want the
discharge summary needs to convey and how to convey that information.
Doubleday Centre Medical Schools Patient Partnership Collaboration
The Doubleday Centre Medical Schools Patient Partnership Collaboration is a network of UK medical schools.
Their key focus is to extend the learning of medical students through partnership with patients and the public. I
have worked with the Doubleday Centre for over 5 years initially as a student ambassador and now as a trustee
for the charity and as a junior doctor representative in the Doubleday Centre Medical Schools Patient Partnership
Collaboration. They will support access to medical school curriculums in WP2 and be invited to WP3 knowledge
share event as an important stakeholder in the dissemination of the findings.
Host Organisation Support Statement
The following statement of support must be completed by the head of your lead academic department. In order to
contribute to this application, this individual must be invited to do so via the 'Participants and Signatories' section of
the form.
Statement of support
I’m writing to offer my strongest support for Dr Calisha Allen and her application for an NIHR Doctoral Fellowship.
As Director of the Institute of Health Informatics (IHI), I can confirm that, if awarded, we will host this fellowship in
the Institute and as primary supervisor I will ensure that we provide a supportive and nurturing environment for
both the project and for Dr Allen’s proposed research training.
I first met Dr Allen 6 months ago when she started her placement as an Academic Clinical Fellow in public health.
I have been extremely impressed with how she has developed her proposal. This has included working night shifts
in a care home to gain experience of what it is like to be a carer and independently establishing a diverse, multi-
disciplinary team of collaborators (within and external to UCL) to support the delivery of each of her studies. Dr
Allen has a very strong track record (including awards) in patient engagement and involvement, and she now
wants to apply what she has learned from working with patients and carers in a research context. In my view she
has huge potential as a public health academic as she is bright, tenacious and extremely committed. Her proposed
work on health literacy will also bring a new dimension to our growing programme of research at the interface
between health and social care.
The UCL Institute of Health Informatics (IHI) is ideally placed to support Dr Allen’s training and development as a
public health academic working at the interface between health and social care. We have world-class expertise in
data science, public health, clinical medicine, health informatics, statistics and AI, and we work closely with social
scientists in the Institute of Global Health and the Centre for Behaviour change. This which will ensure that Dr
Allen has access to the required inter-disciplinary skills to deliver each of her proposed research projects. Her
PhD will be hosted within the VIVALDI social care research programme (https://www.ucl.ac.uk/health-
informatics/research/vivaldi/vivaldi-social-care-study), which is underpinned by a large programme of engagement
with residents, their families and care home staff. This will ensure she has access to a large number of ‘research
engaged’ care homes to facilitate her research. Access to hospital clinicians and discharge summaries at UCLH
and partner hospitals will be facilitated by IHI’s strong links to UCLH via the Computational Medicine theme of the
UCLH Biomedical Research Centre.
As a trainee data scientist at UCL, Dr Allen will benefit from collaborative opportunities aligned to the UCL Grand
Challenge ‘Data Empowered Societies’, which aims to leverage the transformative power of data to address
complex societal challenges. She will also have access to training opportunities and conferences that are hosted
by the NIHR Applied Research Collaborative North Thames which has a specific focus on population health and
social care.
The UCL IHI has an excellent track record of supporting early career researchers. Since 1999, we have welcomed
over 400 overseas and UK postgraduate students from more than 30 countries. It offers a range of courses
including Postgraduate Certificates, Diplomas, MSc, MRes, PhD programmes, and short courses. UCL has an
active programme of support for early career researchers. The UCL Graduate School offers a comprehensive
skills development programme for early career researchers and the Academic Careers Office offers an excellent
programme of training, support and mentorship including the Adapt to Thrive programme. Research staff and
students in the Institute have many opportunities through peer engagement for pastoral support and academic
advice from colleagues across the Faculty of Population Health. Dr Allen will also have regular opportunities to
present at internal research seminars, discuss her research ideas and benefit from the considerable expertise
available among the members of the Institute.
UCL is also well positioned to support Dr Allen in learning about and applying to academic opportunities after
completing the doctoral fellowship. UCL has specific schemes aimed at supporting researchers to transition from
PhD to post-doctoral positions. The UCL Excellence Fellowships (https://www.ucl.ac.uk/school-life-medical-
sciences/research/ucl-excellence-fellowships) provide funding for calibre early career researchers enabling them
to establish themselves at UCL, consolidate their position and develop funding proposals for post-doctoral awards.
I have personally supported PhD students and clinicians to progress their research careers. This includes Dr Maria
Krutikov, Wellcome Trust Clinical Research Fellow who, upon completion of her doctoral fellowship, received
funding from my NIHR Research Professorship (CHERISH) as a Clinical lecturer for a period of 4 years. Dr Steve
Harris, a Consultant in Critical Care also received substantive funding from UCL as a direct result of CHERISH
and is now Chief Research Information Officer at University College Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust. UCL and
UCLH have a strong track record in obtaining funding from the NIHR BRC scheme, with funds flowing to IHI via
the Computational Medicine theme of the BRC. Both in the current award (starting Nov 2022) and the previous
award (2017-2022), we have used BRC funding to support ECRs, in a number of including providing them with
bridging funding whilst applicants apply got clinical lectureship posts
UCL holds a Silver Athena Swan award and holds a Race Equality Charter bronze award. The IHI has a strong
and active Equality, Diversity, and Inclusion Committee that works to continuously improve our environment, and
ensure that we attract and maintain a diverse student body and workforce, embedded in an inclusive culture. We
have an ethnically diverse senior leadership team and actively seek to rotate senior leadership roles to widen
opportunities for career progression.
In summary, Dr Allen is an excellent candidate for the NIHR Doctoral fellowship. She will be supported by
multidisciplinary team of academics, social care professionals and care home residents within and external to UCL
to deliver her proposed project and obtain her PhD. The Institute for Health Informatics is fully committed to
supporting Dr Allen in her PhD and her development towards becoming independent researcher in public health.
